content.problemStatement:
The Chef has decided to take a break from the restaurant. Rather than sitting on some boring tropical beach for hours on end, he has decided to join an archaeological dig. His latest discovery is an ancient tablet containing a list of numbers that seem to fit some pattern. At the top of the list are d numbers a0, ..., ad-1, each of which is either 0 or 1. The rest of the list is only partially readable, but it seems to contain an extremely long list of 0/1 numbers x0, x1, ...



Only some of the xi values are readable and the rest are too damaged to make out. The Chef conjectures that these numbers fit the following pattern. For any number n ≥ 0, the Chef conjectures that xn+d = a0xn + ... + ad-1xn+d-1. Because of the nature of this pattern, he is calling the first d integers x0, ..., xd-1 the "seeds". Finally, this ancient civilization appears to be only concerned about whether a number is odd or even. Because of this, his conjecture is that the numbers fit the previous pattern when all results are reduced modulo 2.



Your job is to help the Chef determine the validitiy of his conjecture. Specifically, you are to determine if there is a way to assign either a 0 or a 1 to all unreadable xi entries so that the pattern holds. If so, you must determine if there is more than one way to do this or if there is exactly one way to do this. If there is exactly one way, you must specify the values of the "seeds".
constraints: All a_i and x_j are 0 or 1
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with two integers d and k (1≤d,k≤20). The next line contains d binary integers a0 … a_{d-1}. Then k lines follow; the j‑th line contains two integers i_j and x_j (0≤i_j<2^31, x_j∈{0,1}) describing a readable entry x_{i_j}. The indices i_j are given in strictly increasing order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output one line: - "no solution" if no assignment of the missing xi can satisfy the recurrence modulo 2. - "multiple solutions" if more than one assignment exists. - Otherwise output the d seed values x0 … x_{d-1} separated by a single space (each 0 or 1).

sampleTestCases.output:
Input:
3
2 2
1 1
0 0
2 1
2 2
1 1
0 0
3 0
2 2
1 0
0 0
2 1

Output:
0 1
multiple solutions
no solution

solution.approach: The recurrence is linear over GF(2). Let S_n=[x_n,x_{n+1},…,x_{n+d-1}]^T. Then S_{n+1}=M·S_n where M is the d×d companion matrix built from the coefficients a_i. Consequently S_i=M^i·S_0 and the first component of S_i (which is x_i) equals the first row of M^i multiplied by the seed vector S_0. For every readable entry (i_j,x_j) we obtain a linear equation (first‑row‑of‑M^{i_j})·seeds = x_j (mod 2). Collecting all k equations gives a system A·seeds = b over GF(2) with at most 20 variables. We solve it with Gaussian elimination using bit‑operations. If the system is inconsistent → “no solution”. If the rank is smaller than d → “multiple solutions”. Otherwise the solution is unique and we output the d seed bits. The matrix exponentiation for each i_j is done by binary exponentiation; d≤20 makes O(d^3 log i) trivial.
solution.timeComplexity: O(k·d^3·log max(i_j)) ≤ O(20·20^3·31) ≈ 5·10^5 operations per test case, easily within limits. Overall O(T·d^3·log maxIndex).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(d^2) for the companion matrix and its powers (≤400 integers).
